researchNotes: Strong real evidence — 4 confirmed hits across 2025/2026, including a classic name-confusion trap (S.N. Banerjee vs. the actual first INC president, W.C. Bonnerjee — easy to mix up given the similar surnames). Expanded 2026-07 pass: re-scanned OCR and found one more genuine hit (2017_q1 page 37 Q172, image-verified: 72 delegates at the first INC session), then added standard high-yield INC facts via web research — the Ilbert Bill backdrop, Badruddin Tyabji's first-Muslim-president distinction, the Annie-Besant-vs-Sarojini-Naidu 'first woman president' trap (a genuinely easy mix-up), the Lahore session/Purna Swaraj/Ravi river flag-hoisting, J.B. Kripalani as president at independence, and the Home Rule League's two journals — rounding out session-by-session coverage beyond the original pass's TN-leaning facts. Round-2 pass (2026-07, second expansion): OCR re-scan found no further genuine hits beyond the 4 already used; added organizational/session facts not yet covered — the Safety Valve Theory (and that Lala Lajpat Rai, not Hume, is the one credited with propagating it as a critique), Naoroji's three presidencies and 1906 Swaraj declaration (distinct from 1929's Purna Swaraj), his Drain-of-Wealth theory and Finsbury Central MP seat, the Partition of Bengal/Swadeshi-Boycott backdrop, the Muslim League's 1906 founding, Naram Dal/Garam Dal terminology with the Lal-Bal-Pal trio, the Servants of India Society/Gokhale-as-Gandhi's-guru fact, and the Quit India Movement's launch details — roughly doubling mock questions (16→27) while deliberately avoiding overlap with adjacent topics (Bhagat Singh/satyagraha/Gandhi-Nehru-Bose-Ambedkar/communalism-partition each have their own dedicated topic files).

reading.intro: Fifth topic of Unit III. Real exam evidence favors founder/president-name traps (similar-sounding names swapped) and TN-specific angles on the national movement (language of political meetings, women legislators) over generic INC session-by-session history.

qa.question: Real exam (trap): Who is considered the 'Father of the Indian National Congress', and who was its FIRST president — was it S.N. Banerjee?
qa.answer: A.O. Hume (a retired British civil servant) is considered the Father of the INC. The first president was W.C. Bonnerjee (Womesh Chunder Bonnerjee) — NOT S.N. Banerjee (Surendranath Banerjee), a different, similarly-named contemporary leader. This name mix-up is a real, confirmed exam trap.

qa.question: When and where was the INC founded?
qa.answer: 1885, in Bombay.

qa.question: Real exam: Why did Lokmanya Tilak replace the term 'Swaraj' with 'Home Rule'?
qa.answer: Because the British government found the term 'Swaraj' highly objectionable and considered it seditious, so Tilak rebranded the same demand using the less provocative term 'Home Rule' instead.

qa.question: What was the 'Surat Split' (1907), and what was the 'Lucknow Pact' (1916)?
qa.answer: Surat Split (1907): the INC split into Moderate and Extremist factions at its Surat session. Lucknow Pact (1916): the Moderates and Extremists reunited, AND the INC and the Muslim League agreed to jointly demand self-government from the British.

qa.question: Real exam: Which party started the 'Anti-Non-Cooperation League' in the Madras Presidency?
qa.answer: The Justice Party (opposing Congress's Non-Cooperation Movement in the Madras Presidency, reflecting the Justice Party's distinct, often Congress-opposed, political stance in the Tamil region).

qa.question: Real exam: True or false — INC held its meetings in Tamil as well (to attract common people), while the Justice Party held meetings only in English.
qa.answer: TRUE on both counts — INC used Tamil too, to reach ordinary people, while the Justice Party's meetings were conducted in English only.

qa.question: Real exam: In what year was Dr. Muthulakshmi Reddy elected Deputy Speaker of the (Madras) Legislature?
Real exam: How many delegates attended the INC's first session (1885, Bombay)?
qa.answer: 72 delegates.

qa.question: What was the Ilbert Bill controversy (1883), and why does it matter for INC's origins?
qa.answer: Law Member C.P. Ilbert proposed a bill allowing Indian judges/magistrates to try British subjects in criminal cases. Fierce, often racially-charged opposition from the British/Anglo-Indian community forced the bill's dilution — but the controversy sharply galvanized Indian political consciousness just two years before the INC was founded (1885), making it an important immediate backdrop to INC's origins.

qa.question: Who presided over the INC's 3rd session (Madras, 1887), and what distinction did he hold?
qa.answer: Badruddin Tyabji — the first Muslim president of the Indian National Congress.

qa.question: Trap to watch: Who was the FIRST woman president of the INC, and who was the first INDIAN woman president — are they the same person?
qa.answer: No — Annie Besant (British-born) was the first woman president of the INC, at the 1917 Calcutta session. Sarojini Naidu was the first INDIAN woman president, at the 1925 Kanpur session — a distinct, later 'first'. Don't collapse the two into one fact.

qa.question: What happened at the INC's Lahore session (December 1929)?
qa.answer: Jawaharlal Nehru presided (a generational shift toward younger leadership). On 31 December 1929, the Congress adopted the 'Purna Swaraj' (Complete Independence) resolution, and Nehru hoisted the tricolour flag on the banks of the Ravi river at midnight. The Congress also fixed 26 January as 'Independence Day', to be observed annually from 1930 onward — the same date later chosen for the Constitution's commencement in 1950.

qa.question: Who was the President of the INC at the time of India's independence (1947)?
qa.answer: J.B. Kripalani (Acharya Kripalani).

qa.question: What was the Home Rule League movement (1916), and how does it connect Tilak and Annie Besant?
qa.answer: Two separate but allied Home Rule Leagues were launched in 1916 — Bal Gangadhar Tilak's in Maharashtra/Karnataka, and Annie Besant's in Madras — both demanding self-government (Home Rule) for India within the British Empire. Besant also published the journals 'New India' and 'Commonweal' to promote the movement, alongside Tilak's separate rebranding of 'Swaraj' as 'Home Rule' (already noted above).

qa.question: Round-2 pass — What is the 'Safety Valve Theory' about INC's founding, and who is most associated with propagating it?
qa.answer: The theory claims A.O. Hume (with tacit British encouragement) founded the INC as a controlled 'safety valve' to channel/defuse growing educated-Indian discontent before it turned into revolutionary unrest, rather than out of genuine sympathy for Indian self-rule. Lala Lajpat Rai is most associated with propagating this critique (in his writings on Hume and the INC's origins); the theory is also disputed/discredited by some historians who point to Hume's demonstrable personal sympathy for Indian reform.

qa.question: How many times was Dadabhai Naoroji president of the INC, and at which sessions?
qa.answer: Three times: 1886 (Calcutta), 1893 (Lahore), and 1906 (Calcutta). At the 1906 Calcutta session — held under pressure from the rising Extremist faction — Naoroji formally declared 'Swaraj' (self-government) as the Congress's goal for the first time, an important precursor to the more famous 1929 Lahore 'Purna Swaraj' resolution two decades later.

qa.question: What other major distinctions did Dadabhai Naoroji hold besides his three INC presidencies?
qa.answer: He is known as the 'Grand Old Man of India'; he propounded the 'Drain of Wealth' theory (that British economic policies systematically drained India's wealth to Britain), detailed in his book 'Poverty and Un-British Rule in India'; and he became the first Indian (and first Asian) elected to the British House of Commons, winning the Finsbury Central seat in 1892 as a Liberal Party MP.

qa.question: What was the Partition of Bengal (1905), and how did the INC respond?
qa.answer: Viceroy Lord Curzon partitioned Bengal in 1905, officially for administrative convenience but widely seen as a 'divide and rule' move to weaken Bengali political unity. The INC responded by backing the Swadeshi (buy Indian-made goods) and Boycott (of British goods) movements, formally endorsing them at its 1905 Benares session and pushing them further at the 1906 Calcutta session (the same session where Naoroji declared the Swaraj goal).

qa.question: When and where was the All-India Muslim League founded, and by whom, in relation to the INC?
qa.answer: Founded in 1906 at Dhaka (in present-day Bangladesh), initially to safeguard separate Muslim political interests (including separate electorates) — distinct from, and at times in tension with, the INC's more broadly 'national' platform, though the two briefly allied via the 1916 Lucknow Pact.

qa.question: What were the Naram Dal and Garam Dal within the INC, and who made up the famous Extremist trio?
qa.answer: Naram Dal ('Moderates') favoured gradual constitutional reform through petitions and dialogue with the British; Garam Dal ('Extremists') demanded more assertive, direct methods (boycott, Swadeshi, mass mobilization) toward Swaraj. The Extremist trio was popularly called 'Lal-Bal-Pal': Lala Lajpat Rai, Bal Gangadhar Tilak, and Bipin Chandra Pal.

qa.question: What was the Servants of India Society, and what was Gopal Krishna Gokhale's relationship to both Tilak and Gandhi?
qa.answer: Gokhale founded the Servants of India Society in 1905 to train selfless 'national missionaries' for public service and moderate constitutional reform. Gokhale, a leading Moderate, was a rival of the Extremist Tilak within the INC, but is separately and famously remembered as Mahatma Gandhi's political guru/mentor after Gandhi's return to India from South Africa.

qa.question: What was the Quit India Movement, when was it launched, and what was its rallying slogan?
qa.answer: Launched by the INC on 8 August 1942 via a resolution passed at the Bombay (Gowalia Tank Maidan) session of the All India Congress Committee, demanding an immediate end to British rule. Gandhi's rallying call was 'Do or Die' ('Karo ya Maro'). The British responded by immediately arresting almost the entire top Congress leadership.

questions.prompt: Who is considered the 'Father of the Indian National Congress'?
questions.explanation: A.O. Hume.

questions.prompt: Who was the FIRST president of the Indian National Congress?
questions.explanation: W.C. Bonnerjee — not S.N. Banerjee, a common name-confusion trap.

questions.prompt: Why did Lokmanya Tilak rebrand 'Swaraj' as 'Home Rule'?
options: Home Rule was a Congress tradition
questions.explanation: The British government found 'Swaraj' objectionable/seditious.

questions.prompt: Which party started the 'Anti-Non-Cooperation League' in the Madras Presidency?
questions.explanation: The Justice Party.

questions.prompt: Which statement is correct about political meetings in the Madras Presidency era?
options: INC held meetings only in English
Justice Party held meetings in Tamil to reach common people
INC held meetings in Tamil too, to attract common people
questions.explanation: INC used Tamil as well as English to reach ordinary people; the Justice Party's meetings were English-only.

questions.prompt: Dr. Muthulakshmi Reddy was elected Deputy Speaker of the Madras Legislature in which year?
The INC split into Moderate and Extremist factions at which 1907 session, known as the:
questions.explanation: The Surat Split.

questions.prompt: The Indian National Congress was founded in which year and city?
questions.explanation: 1885, Bombay.

questions.prompt: Real exam: How many delegates attended the INC's first session (1885, Bombay)?
questions.explanation: 72 delegates.

questions.prompt: The Ilbert Bill controversy, which sharply galvanized Indian political consciousness just before INC's founding, occurred in which year?
The INC's 3rd session (Madras, 1887) was presided over by Badruddin Tyabji, who was notable as:
options: The first Muslim president of the INC
The founder of the Muslim League
The first Congress president from Madras
questions.explanation: The first Muslim president of the INC.

questions.prompt: Who was the FIRST woman president of the Indian National Congress (1917, Calcutta session)?
questions.explanation: Annie Besant — not Sarojini Naidu, a common trap.

questions.prompt: Who was the first INDIAN woman president of the INC, at the 1925 Kanpur session?
questions.explanation: Sarojini Naidu — distinct from Annie Besant, the first woman president overall (1917).

questions.prompt: At the INC's Lahore session (December 1929), presided over by Nehru, the tricolour flag was hoisted on the banks of which river as the 'Purna Swaraj' resolution was adopted?
questions.explanation: The Ravi river, at Lahore, on the night of 31 December 1929.

questions.prompt: Who was the President of the Indian National Congress at the time of India's independence (1947)?
questions.explanation: J.B. Kripalani (Acharya Kripalani).

questions.prompt: Annie Besant's 1916 Home Rule League (based in Madras) promoted its cause through which two journals?
questions.explanation: New India and Commonweal (Tilak's separate Home Rule League used 'Kesari'/'Mahratta', which are distractors here).

questions.prompt: The 'Safety Valve Theory' about INC's founding is most associated with being propagated by:
questions.explanation: Lala Lajpat Rai is most associated with propagating this critique of Hume's motives in founding the INC.

questions.prompt: Dadabhai Naoroji served as INC president three times, at the sessions of:
questions.explanation: 1886 (Calcutta), 1893 (Lahore), and 1906 (Calcutta).

questions.prompt: At the 1906 Calcutta session, Dadabhai Naoroji formally declared which goal for the INC for the first time?
questions.explanation: 'Swaraj' — note this is distinct from and earlier than the 1929 Lahore session's more specific 'Purna Swaraj' resolution.
questions.trick: 1906 Calcutta = first formal 'Swaraj' demand (Naoroji); 1929 Lahore = 'Purna Swaraj'/complete independence resolution (Nehru) — two distinct milestones, don't merge them.

questions.prompt: Dadabhai Naoroji's 'Drain of Wealth' theory, detailed in his book 'Poverty and Un-British Rule in India', argued that:
options: British policies systematically drained India's wealth to Britain
Indian princely states were wealthier than British India
Agricultural taxes should be abolished entirely
questions.explanation: British economic policies systematically drained India's wealth to Britain.

questions.prompt: Dadabhai Naoroji became the first Indian elected to the British House of Commons in 1892, representing which constituency?
questions.explanation: Finsbury Central, as a Liberal Party MP.

questions.prompt: The Partition of Bengal (1905), carried out by Viceroy Lord Curzon, directly provoked which two linked INC-backed movements?
questions.explanation: Swadeshi (buy Indian goods) and Boycott (of British goods), endorsed at the 1905 Benares and 1906 Calcutta sessions.

questions.prompt: The All-India Muslim League was founded in 1906 at:
questions.explanation: Dhaka (present-day Bangladesh).

questions.prompt: Within the INC, the 'Naram Dal' and 'Garam Dal' referred respectively to the:
questions.explanation: Naram Dal = Moderates (gradual reform); Garam Dal = Extremists (direct assertive methods).

questions.prompt: The Extremist trio popularly known as 'Lal-Bal-Pal' consisted of Lala Lajpat Rai, Bipin Chandra Pal, and:
questions.explanation: Bal Gangadhar Tilak.

questions.prompt: Gopal Krishna Gokhale, founder of the Servants of India Society (1905), is separately and famously remembered as:
questions.explanation: Mahatma Gandhi's political guru/mentor after his return from South Africa.

questions.prompt: The Quit India Movement was launched by an INC resolution on 8 August 1942 at which session, with what rallying slogan?
options: Bombay (Gowalia Tank Maidan) session; 'Do or Die'
questions.explanation: The Bombay AICC session at Gowalia Tank Maidan; Gandhi's call was 'Do or Die' ('Karo ya Maro').
